entry.title: Hello World
entry.content:
This is the first post on a new blog. The theme is built for long-form writing — clean typography, no distractions, and a layout that gets out of the way.

How it works

Posts are written in Markdown and live in the _posts directory. Jekyll handles the rest: parsing front matter, generating pages, building the RSS feed.

The design leans on two typefaces. IBM Plex Mono handles headings, navigation, labels — anything structural. IBM Plex Serif handles body text, where readability matters most.

What belongs here

Notes on things worth writing about. Analysis when something deserves more than a quick take. The occasional long read when the subject earns it.

The best writing happens when you stop trying to sound like a writer and start trying to be clear.

That’s the goal.
entry.summary: This is the first post on a new blog. The theme is built for long-form writing — clean typography, no distractions, and a layout that gets out of the way.

entry.title: On Writing Clearly
entry.content:
Clear writing starts with clear thinking. If you cannot explain what you mean in a plain sentence, adding more words will not help. It will make things worse.

Cut the filler

Every sentence should carry weight. If a sentence exists only to transition between two other sentences, delete it and let the gap do the work. Readers are smarter than most writing gives them credit for.

Say the thing

The most common failure mode in writing is circling the point without landing on it. State the claim. Support it. Move on.

This applies whether you are writing a memo, a blog post, or an email. The reader’s time is not yours to waste.

Read it out loud

If a sentence sounds wrong when spoken, it reads wrong too. The ear catches what the eye skips — awkward rhythm, unnecessary repetition, clauses that go nowhere.

None of this is new advice. The hard part is doing it consistently, especially when the subject is complex and the temptation to hedge is strong.
entry.summary: Clear writing starts with clear thinking. If you cannot explain what you mean in a plain sentence, adding more words will not help. It will make things worse.
